turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: The second case I have to announce is number 24-7, Diamond Alternative Energy versus Environmental Protection Agency. Several years ago, under a provision of the Clean Air Act, the U.S Environmental Protection Agency or EPA approved California regulations requiring automakers to alter their fleets of new vehicles. Under those California regulations, automakers must manufacture more electric vehicles and fewer gasoline-powered vehicles. The goal is to decrease greenhouse gas and other emissions from the use of gasoline and other liquid fuels in cars. Producers of gasoline and other liquid fuels sued EPA, arguing that EPA's approval of the California regulations violated the Clean Air Act. The sole issue before this Court is a preliminary one, whether the fuel producers have standing to maintain their suit in court. The U.S Court of Appeals for the D.C. Circuit held that the fuel producers lack standing to sue. We disagree. The fuel producers have standing, and we therefore reverse the judgment of the D.C. Circuit. Article 3 of the Constitution confines the jurisdiction of federal courts to cases and controversies. For a lawsuit to constitute a case within the meaning of Article 3, the plaintiffs must have standing to sue. To demonstrate standing, plaintiffs must answer a basic question, what's it to you? In other words, plaintiffs must show that they possess a personal stake in the dispute and are not mere bystanders. This Court's cases have established that standing contains three elements, injury in fact, causation, and redressability. Here, as for injury in fact, the fuel producers make money by selling fuel. Therefore, a decrease in purchases of gasoline and other liquid fuels resulting from the California regulation hurts their bottom-line. Those monetary costs are of course an injury in fact. As to causation and redressability, common sense economic principles demonstrate that the fuel producers have satisfied those standing requirements as well. The California regulations force automakers to manufacture more electric vehicles and fewer gasoline-powered vehicles. That in turn means fewer consumer purchases of gasoline and other liquid fuels, which of course causes monetary harm to the fuel producers. And invalidating the regulations would redress the fuel producers' monetary injuries because more gasoline-powered cars would be manufactured and sold, meaning that more gasoline and other liquid fuel would be purchased. All of that is fairly commonsensical. Still, EPA and California push back on that reasoning, asserting that this case is unusual. They primarily suggest that the new vehicle market has developed in a way that even if the California regulations are invalidated, automakers would not likely manufacture or sell more gasoline-powered cars than they do now. So, they argue that the fuel producers have not established redressability. They say that the fuel producers' injuries would not be redressed even if the California regulations were invalidated. To begin with, that is an odd argument for EPA and California to advance to try to defeat standing. After all, if invalidating the regulations would change nothing in the market, why are EPA and California enforcing and defending the regulations? The whole point of the regulations is to increase the number of electric vehicles in the new automobile market beyond what consumers would otherwise demand and what automakers would otherwise manufacture and sell. And EPA and California have been tenaciously defending the regulations for several years, presumably because they think the regulations still make a difference in the market. In any event, the evidence in the record, in this case, confirms what common sense tells us. Invalidating the regulations likely would make a difference for the fuel producers because automakers would likely manufacture more vehicles that run on gasoline and other liquid fuels. So, there would be an increase in purchases of gasoline and other liquid fuels from the fuel producers. Consider just a few examples of record evidence. When originally seeking EPA's approval for the regulations, California itself estimated that its standards would produce substantial reductions in demand for gasoline, exceeding 1 billion beginning in 2020, and increasing to over $10 billion in 2030. Around that time, EPA likewise acknowledged that the California regulations would reduce consumption of fuel and credited California's estimates that the regulations would continue reducing greenhouse gas emissions in California through at least 2037. Moreover, five automakers predicted that absent California's regulations, other automakers would seek a competitive advantage over them by selling fewer electric vehicles and more gasoline-powered vehicles. In short, EPAs and California's own actions, their statements, their enforcement decisions, and their litigation positions, as well as other evidence in the record, undermines the central premise of their redressability argument. When as here a government seeks to justify its regulatory actions by, on the one hand, touting the consequences for fuel usage and emissions, while on the other, maintaining that those same regulations are unreviewable in court because there are no consequences, courts can appropriately be skeptical. In the words of one of this Court's prior cases, judges are not required to exhibit a naivete from which ordinary citizens are free. In sum, as this Court has said before, courts should not make standing law more complicated than it needs to be. The government generally may not target a business or industry through stringent and allegedly unlawful regulation and then evade the resulting lawsuits by claiming that the targets of its regulation should be locked out of court as unaffected bystanders. In light of this Court's precedents and the evidence before the Court of Appeals, the fuel producers here established Article III standing to challenge EPA's approval of the California regulations. To be clear, this case concerns only standing, not the merits. EPA and California may or may not prevail against the fuel producers on the merits in defending EPA's approval of the California regulations. But the fuel producers clearly have standing to sue and have the merits of their legal arguments decided by the federal courts. For those reasons, we reverse the judgment of the U.S Court of Appeals for the D.C. Circuit. Justice Sotomayor and Justice Jackson have each filed dissenting opinions.
